content.text: Our sense of touch is subjective. For example, if you dip one hand in warm water and another in ice-cold water, and then both in tap water, each hand will perceive the tap water temperature differently. Thus, touch is not a reliable measure of hotness or coldness.

content.title: Activity 7.1: Testing Sense of Touch
content.content: Using three containers (Warm, Tap, and Ice-cold), this activity proves that the SAME water can feel both cool and warm simultaneously to different hands.

content.text: Temperature is a reliable measure of the hotness or coldness of a body. A thermometer is the device used for this measurement.
Used to measure human body temperature. Types include: 1) Mercury (toxic, traditional), 2) Digital (uses heat sensors, safer), and 3) Infrared (non-contact, measures from a distance).

content.title: Clinical Range
content.text: Human body temperature normally stays between 35°C and 42°C.
Composed of a long glass tube with a bulb containing alcohol or mercury. They have a wider range (-10°C to 110°C).

content.title: Example 1
content.description: If a laboratory thermometer has 10 small divisions between 0°C and 10°C, find the value of one small division.
content.solution:
1) Identify the difference between marks: 10°C - 0°C = 10°C.
2) Count the number of divisions: 10.
3) Calculate (Difference ÷ Divisions): 10 ÷ 10 = 1°C.
Each small division reads 1°C.

content.text:
1) Celsius Scale (°C): Most common for clinical/daily use. Normal body temp = 37.0°C.
2) Fahrenheit Scale (°F): Normal body temp = 98.6°F.
3) Kelvin Scale (K): The SI unit of temperature. It has no degree (°) sign.

content.title: Example 2
content.description: Convert a normal human body temperature of 37°C into the Kelvin scale.
content.solution:
Formula: Temp in K = Temp in °C + 273.15.
Calculation: 37 + 273.15 = 310.15 K.
The temperature is 310.15 K.

content.title: Example 3
content.description: Komal mentions she has a '101 degree' fever. On which scale is she likely measuring, and why?
content.solution: She is using the Fahrenheit scale (°F). On the Celsius scale, body temperature does not go above 42°C. A reading of 101°C would be boiling water temperature, which is impossible for a human body.

content.title: Measuring with Lab Thermometer
content.content: Keep the thermometer vertical. Bulb must not touch sides/bottom of container. Read while immersed. Eye level must align with the liquid column top.

content.title: Thermal Constants
content.text: Water temperature remains constant at 100°C while boiling and at 0°C (ice) while melting.
Lowest possible temperature is -273.15°C (0 K), called Absolute Zero. The Sun's core can reach 15 million °C; there is no upper limit to temperature.

glossary.definition: A device used to measure temperature accurately.
A scale where 37.0 is normal body temperature.
A non-contact device that measures temperature from a distance.

summary_points: Temperature provides a reliable measure of hotness/coldness (unlike sense of touch).
Clinical thermometers measure body temp (35°C to 42°C); Normal is ~37.0°C.
Laboratory thermometers measure wider ranges (-10°C to 110°C) and must be read while immersed.
SI unit of temperature is Kelvin (K). Conversion: K = °C + 273.15.
Anna Mani was an eminent Indian meteorologist known for her work on weather instruments.
